sourceMovie.title: Shrek
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: People revisit Shrek for the same reason it broke through in 2001: clear stakes, memorable faces, and a comedy rhythm that doesn't stall in the middle. Andrew Adamson keeps the ensemble pointed in one direction. The animation and comedy blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Shrek Forever After
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Shrek clicked for you, Shrek Forever After (2010) is a strong next stop: both trade in animation and comedy, with comic timing and sharper banter. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: Puss in Boots
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Shrek clicked for you, Puss in Boots (2011) is a strong next stop: both trade in animation and comedy, with comic timing and sharper banter. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Shrek?
faq.answer: Try Shrek Forever After, Puss in Boots, The Swan Princess—each shares DNA with Shrek while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Shrek?
faq.answer: Shrek (2001) is best described as Animation, Comedy, Fantasy, Adventure, Family. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Shrek part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Shrek Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
